one_line_takeaway: Ten state-of-the-art black-box LLM fingerprinting schemes that claim benign robustness fall to cheap adaptive attacks by a white-box malicious host -- near-100% verification-evasion at <10% utility loss for memorization/GCG schemes -- while only watermark-based fingerprints partially resist (65% ASR).
key_finding: Across ten recently proposed black-box fingerprinting schemes, tailored adaptive attacks achieve almost perfect ASR while keeping high benign utility. Per Table 1, ASR reaches 100% for ChainHash, FPEdit, ImF, Perinucleus, InstructionalFP (InstrFP), MergePrint, RoFL and ProFLingo; 94% for EditMF; and 65% for Domain-Specific Watermarks. Memorization-based schemes are the most fragile because exact memorization causes both verbatim-verification and token overconfidence; schemes that move away from exact memorization/regurgitation (EditMF's knowledge-editing with multi-paraphrase verification, and the KGW-watermark scheme) are notably harder to break. The watermark scheme is the most robust but still leaks: even prompting with a BOS token plus a common English word (a domain mismatch), ~35% of generations had p-value < 0.01, enabling watermark stealing. The paper concludes fingerprints must be designed for adversarial robustness by design and offers four recommendations (natural-looking queries, stealthy output logits, no reliance on exact memorization/regurgitation, and mutually independent fingerprints not discoverable by prompting).
core_contribution: This is a red-team / attack paper (not a new fingerprinting method) arguing that existing black-box LLM fingerprinting schemes claim 'robustness' only in a benign sense (fine-tuning, merging, prompt-wrappers) and are not adversarially robust against a malicious model host. The authors' own contributions are threefold: (1) a concrete, practical threat model for model fingerprinting under an adversarial API host; (2) identification of four fundamental vulnerabilities each shared across a family of schemes -- verbatim verification of exactly-memorized responses, overconfidence of memorized fingerprint tokens, unnatural (high-perplexity) GCG-generated fingerprint queries, and leaked statistical signatures in watermark-based fingerprints; and (3) four families of adaptive attacks (output-suppression, output-detection, input-detection, and statistical/watermark-stealing) that bypass verification on ten recently proposed schemes while preserving benign model utility. The surveyed schemes themselves are prior work / attack targets, NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: An adversarial model host possesses the white-box weights of a fingerprinted model and serves it to users via an API; the legitimate model owner can only verify ownership through black-box API queries. Assumptions stated by the paper: the fingerprinting protocol/algorithm is public and reproducible by the adversary, but the secret randomness used to generate a specific fingerprint is hidden; the adversary cannot run a different LLM of comparable quality to substitute responses (otherwise stealing the model would be pointless); the adversary has unfettered white-box access and can run/modify the model offline. Adversary goal: return useful responses to benign users while causing fingerprint verification to fail. Success is measured by Attack Success Rate (ASR = fraction of fingerprint queries that fail to verify), traded off against Normalized Utility relative to the base model.
limitations: Stated by the authors: they consider only instruction-tuned chatbot-style models, not tool-use or reasoning/'thinking' models; they focus on black-box fingerprinting and do not attack white-box/grey-box schemes (DeepJudge, REEF, HUREF, etc.); and some methods that do not share these four vulnerabilities are out of scope, e.g. the rank-based uniformity-test intrinsic fingerprint. Reviewer-noted: the statistical/watermark attack assumes white-box weight access and log-prob availability plus a calibration model sharing the tokenizer, and only reaches 65% ASR, so the strongest results (100% ASR) are concentrated on the more brittle memorization/intrinsic families; utility is measured on base rather than fingerprinted models to isolate the attack's effect.

datasets_used: WildChat (real user-chatbot queries, used as benign distribution for perplexity input-detection)
Instruction-tuned Llama-3 and Qwen2.5 models at ~1B and ~8B sizes, with 16 or 128 embedded fingerprints
quantitative_results: Table 1 ASR by scheme: ChainHash 100%, FPEdit 100%, ImF 100%, EditMF 94%, Perinucleus 100%, InstrFP 100%, MergePrint 100%, ROFL 100%, ProFLingo 100%, DSWatermark 65%

InstructionalFP: SuppressTop-k trivially gives 100% ASR under token-level MatchPrefix; stronger ST/SN reach 100% ASR even under lenient MatchKeyword with <5% relative utility drop; effectiveness drops from 100% (no attack) to 12.5% under SuppressLookahead (Fig 2)
Chain&Hash (128 fp): SuppressNeighbor achieves 100% ASR under MatchPrefix and 95% ASR under the stricter MatchSubstring, with <6% utility drop
Perinucleus (16 fp): SN reaches 100% ASR under MatchSubstring with <5% utility drop
Implicit FP (16 fp): ST achieves perfect ASR under MatchSubstring at ~5% utility drop, and perfect ASR under ROUGE-L>0.9 at <10% relative utility drop
FPEdit (16 fp, knowledge editing): SN achieves ~90% ASR at ~10% utility drop (less overconfidence makes detection harder)
EditMF (16 fp): SuppressLookahead reaches 100% ASR with single-query detection; ~95% ASR (Qwen-1.5B) and ~70% (Llama) under multi-paraphrase MK-Multi with minimal utility loss; SN is ineffective here

RoFL / ProFLingo / MergePrint (128 fp, GCG intrinsic): perplexity filtering with Llama-3.2-1B gives 100% ASR with no utility drop; GPT-2 filtering suffices for RoFL on Llama and for MergePrint; near-perfect ROC separation of WildChat vs 128 RoFL fingerprints

Domain-Specific Watermarks (Llama-3.2-1B): 65% ASR at 92% relative utility; median verification p-value shifts from ~1e-11 (no attack) to ~0.05 under attack (200 tokens/query, 100 queries); ~35% of BOS+common-word generations still had p-value < 0.01, showing watermark leakage
